summary: Cumulative audit on the Variable + IRawNameResolvable migration (coder/v5 -> v7 + commit 4). Codeanalyzer/v4, tester/v7, and security/v2 each PASSed; security caught the dominant cross-cutting issue but rated it low under the user-sovereign threat model. Auditor escalates: the deleted RawScalarValidations safety net is not replaced by [IsNotNull] anywhere on the 22 Data<Variable> slots (security said 3 of 22 but those decorations are on OTHER properties in the same handler, not the Variable slot). Pre-v7 produced a clear ServiceError("...nothing to use as 'name'...", "MissingParameter", 400) when a [VariableName] slot was missing or null; post-v7 the same case throws NullReferenceException at op_Implicit, escapes App.Run's NRE-excluded catch, and is caught by Step.RunAsync as a generic ServiceError("Object reference not set...", "StepError", 400). The regression replaces a domain-aware diagnostic with a stack-derived one; the parameter name + step text + Params snapshot are all lost. Verdict FAIL on one MAJOR (cross-file contract regression) + 2 MINOR (review-gap findings explaining how the major slipped through).

previous_reviews_assessed.codeanalyzer: partial — file-level review was clean (3 MINOR + 7 NIT all valid), but missed the architect/v5-vs-implementation gap that lives between the architect plan and the per-handler [IsNotNull] decorator distribution. Not codeanalyzer's job by character (file-by-file scope), but worth flagging.
previous_reviews_assessed.tester: partial — deletion-tested the carve-out (35 tests strongly load-bearing; verdict is real for the migration's positive path) and caught the misnamed PLNG001 test, the WasPercentWrapped value-only pinning, and the variable.set CopyProperties C# coverage gap. Did not write a regression test for the missing-parameter graceful-error contract that pre-v7 had.
previous_reviews_assessed.security: partial — accurately identified the bug in finding #1 with a precise exploit_sketch, correct affected_files list, and the right generator-side fix proposal. Severity rating (low) is defensible under the user-sovereign threat model but understates the contract regression. Counted [IsNotNull] handlers (3 of 22) using a too-loose predicate (decoration anywhere in the handler, not on the Variable slot).

findings.issue: Missing-parameter diagnostic regression across 22 migrated handlers. Pre-v7 the source generator emitted a RawScalarValidations block that produced ServiceError("'<value>' is empty - nothing to use as '<name>' in step: <step text>", "MissingParameter", 400) when a [VariableName] string slot was null/empty. That block was deleted in coder/v7 commit 3 on the architect/v5 plan's assumption that [IsNotNull] would cover the case. Implementation reality: ZERO of 22 Data<Variable> slots carry [IsNotNull] — the only [IsNotNull] decorations on migrated handlers are on OTHER properties (any.cs Key+Operator, group.cs Key). Post-v7 path for a missing or null Name slot: __ResolveData -> NotFound (Value=null) -> AsT_Impl<Variable>(null, ctx) skips the bypass branch (raw is not string), falls through every other branch to WrapAs(null, ctx) -> ConstructWrap<Variable>(default, ctx) -> Data<Variable>{Value=null, Success=true}. Generated property getter checks `if (!Backing.Success) __resolutionError = Backing` — Success is true so __resolutionError stays null. Handler reads Name.Value (null Variable), op_Implicit dereferences v.Name on null instance, NullReferenceException. App.Run line 415 catch deliberately excludes NRE so it propagates out of dispatch; Step.RunAsync line 157 catch does NOT exclude NRE so the step catches it and produces ServiceError(ex.Message="Object reference not set to an instance of an object.", "StepError", 400) — without parameter name, without step text, without Params snapshot. Same shape across variable.{get,set,exists,clear,remove}, list.{add,any/ListName,contains,count,first,flatten,get,group/ListName,indexof,join,last,remove,reverse,set,sort,unique}.
findings.impact: Robustness regression: a clear domain-aware error message ("missing parameter <name> for step <text>") becomes a stack-derived generic StepError("Object reference not set..."). Loses: parameter name, step context, Params snapshot, the MissingParameter error key, error categorization. Builders, tests, and human debuggers seeing this error post-v7 cannot tell from the message which parameter is missing; pre-v7 they could. Trust boundary holds (.pr signed) so this is not an external-attacker vector — but it is a developer-experience and diagnostics regression that the architect's plan explicitly intended to prevent.
findings.suggestion: Generator-side fix at PLang.Generators/Emission/Property/Data/this.cs:EmitProperty. Plumb a new IsRawNameResolvable flag through ActionClassInfo from Discovery (mirror IsSensitive's pattern). For non-nullable Data<T> where T : IRawNameResolvable, emit after the existing __resolutionError capture: `if (Backing.Value == null) __resolutionError = Data.@this.FromError(new ServiceError($"Required parameter '{ParamName}' is missing or null", __step, __callFrames, "MissingRequiredParameter", 400));`. Roughly 10 lines of generator + 1 Discovery flag + 1 ActionClassInfo field. Per-handler [IsNotNull] x22 is the alternative but brittle (next migrated handler will forget). Empirically verified during audit: NotFound.As<Variable>(ctx).Success is true; Value is null; op_Implicit on null Variable throws NRE.

findings.issue: security/v2 finding #1 cites '3 of 22' handlers having [IsNotNull]. The actual count on Data<Variable> slots specifically is ZERO of 22. The 3 handlers cited (any.Key+Operator, group.Key) carry [IsNotNull] on OTHER (non-Variable) properties — those decorations don't help when the Variable slot is missing because the [IsNotNull] check loops over IsNotNullProperties only. Security's count understates the regression's blast radius and contributes to the low severity rating.
findings.impact: Severity-rating inputs based on 'most handlers are guarded' are wrong. Reading the security report leaves the impression a few stragglers need [IsNotNull]; the actual state is no Variable slot has it. This compounds the codeanalyzer + tester gap into a finding that looks 'mostly fine' instead of 'systematic'.
findings.suggestion: When security or any reviewer counts decorator coverage on a specific property kind, the predicate should match the property kind exactly (here: [IsNotNull] on Data<Variable> slots), not 'handler has [IsNotNull] anywhere'. Future cross-spec reviews would benefit from grep patterns in result.md so the predicate is auditable.

findings.issue: tester/v7 deletion-tested the carve-out itself (35 tests strongly load-bearing — confirmed) but did not write a regression test for the missing-parameter graceful-error path. Pre-v7 invariant: missing/null [VariableName] slot returns ServiceError with the MissingParameter key. Post-v7: NRE -> StepError. A 1-test pin asserting `result.Error.Key == "MissingParameter"` (or whatever the post-fix key is) on each migrated handler would have caught the regression at v7 commit 3 land time, not after security/v2's red-team pass.
findings.impact: When the safety net is replaced by reviewer-time check rather than test-time check, the regression bypasses the test suite entirely. C# 2550/2550 green is honest but does not pin the deleted contract. The 4 minor findings tester/v7 filed (CopyProperties coverage, IRawNameResolvable contract trap, misnamed PLNG001 test, WasPercentWrapped) are valid but adjacent — none cover the central regression.
findings.suggestion: When this finding is closed (per finding #1's generator-side fix), add a single test per migrated handler asserting `Action.Run() with missing required Name -> result.Error.Key == "MissingRequiredParameter"`. Bulk-parametrize via TUnit's [Arguments] over the 22 handlers if that fits the test style. Same shape would have caught this at coder/v7 commit 3.
